name: Experimental Psychology
description: An introduction to the scientific methods, research designs, statistical tools, and ethical standards used to conduct and evaluate psychological research.

modules.title: Science & the Scientific Method
modules.objectives: Describe the goals and characteristics of science, explain the steps of the scientific method, and distinguish science from pseudoscience.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2 — for background reading on Science & the Scientific Method before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Identify a popular claim in the media (e.g., a wellness trend). Apply the steps of the scientific method to evaluate it: state a testable hypothesis, describe how you could test it, and identify what evidence would support or refute it. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Science & the Scientific Method module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Research Ethics
modules.objectives: Explain the key principles of ethical research (informed consent, deception, debriefing, confidentiality), and evaluate landmark ethical violations in psychology.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.4 — for background reading on Research Ethics before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Analyze the Tuskegee Syphilis Study or the Stanford Prison Experiment. Identify three specific ethical violations using APA guidelines and explain what safeguards modern IRBs would require for a similar study today. Submit your work as a labeled psychology artifact that includes: (1) at least three specific examples or observations, (2) a short explanation linking each example to the relevant concept or theory, (3) one limitation or alternative interpretation, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the idea to school, work, relationships, or personal behavior. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Research Ethics module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Observational Methods
modules.objectives: Distinguish between naturalistic and structured observation, describe participant vs. non-participant observation, and evaluate the strengths and limitations of observational research.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.2 — for background reading on Observational Methods before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Conduct a 20-minute naturalistic observation in a public setting (e.g., a cafeteria or library). Record your observations using a structured coding sheet, then evaluate what the observer effect might have influenced. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Observational Methods module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Survey & Self-Report Methods
modules.objectives: Explain the principles of effective survey design, identify common response biases, and compare open-ended and closed-ended question formats.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.2 — for background reading on Survey & Self-Report Methods before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Design a 10-question survey to study student stress. Include both Likert-scale and open-ended items. Identify two potential sources of bias in your survey and explain how you would minimize them. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Survey & Self-Report Methods module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Correlational Research
modules.objectives: Explain what a correlation coefficient measures, interpret scatter plots, and explain why correlation does not imply causation. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.3 — for background reading on Correlational Research before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Find a real published correlation in psychology (e.g., screen time and depression). Describe the correlation coefficient, evaluate its strength, and generate two plausible third-variable explanations for the relationship. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Correlational Research module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Experimental Design
modules.objectives: Identify the components of a true experiment, distinguish between between-subjects and within-subjects designs, and explain the importance of control groups.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.3 — for background reading on Experimental Design before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Between vs. Within Subjects Designs
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Design a complete experiment testing whether study breaks improve test performance. Specify the IV, DV, control group, participant assignment method, and how you would control for at least two potential confounds. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Experimental Design module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Variables & Control
modules.objectives: Operationally define independent, dependent, and confounding variables, explain experimental control techniques, and recognize sources of bias. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.3 — for background reading on Variables & Control before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Read a summary of a published psychology experiment. Identify the independent variable, dependent variable, and two potential confounding variables. Suggest one specific control procedure for each confound you identify. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Variables & Control module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Sampling Methods
modules.objectives: Compare probability and non-probability sampling techniques, explain sampling error, and evaluate how sample characteristics affect generalizability. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.2 — for background reading on Sampling Methods before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: A researcher wants to study social media use among US high school students. Describe how you would use stratified random sampling to create a representative sample, and explain why simple random sampling might not be sufficient. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Sampling Methods module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a goal of psychological science?
quizQuestions.answer: Entertainment
quizQuestions.explanation: The four goals of psychology are description, explanation, prediction, and control/application — not entertainment.

quizQuestions.question: A testable prediction derived from a theory is called a:
quizQuestions.answer: Hypothesis
quizQuestions.explanation: A hypothesis is a specific, testable prediction about the relationship between variables derived from theory.

quizQuestions.question: Select the best answer: Which of the following is NOT a goal of psychological science?
quizQuestions.answer: Entertainment
quizQuestions.explanation: The four goals of psychology are description, explanation, prediction, and control/application — not entertainment.

quizQuestions.question: Informed consent requires that participants:
options: Are fully deceived about the study's purpose
Are told enough about the study to make a voluntary decision to participate
Cannot withdraw from a study once begun
quizQuestions.answer: Are told enough about the study to make a voluntary decision to participate
quizQuestions.explanation: Informed consent is an ethical requirement that participants receive sufficient information about the study to decide whether to participate voluntarily.

quizQuestions.question: Debriefing in research involves:
options: Explaining the true purpose of the study after it ends
Obtaining consent before the study begins
quizQuestions.answer: Explaining the true purpose of the study after it ends
quizQuestions.explanation: Debriefing informs participants about any deception used and the full purpose of the study, allowing them to make sense of their experience.

quizQuestions.question: The Belmont Report established which three core principles of research ethics?
quizQuestions.answer: Respect, justice, beneficence
quizQuestions.explanation: The Belmont Report (1979) identified three principles: respect for persons (autonomy), beneficence (do good/avoid harm), and justice (fair distribution of benefits and risks).

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Informed consent requires that participants:
options: Are fully deceived about the study's purpose
Are told enough about the study to make a voluntary decision to participate
Cannot withdraw from a study once begun
quizQuestions.answer: Are told enough about the study to make a voluntary decision to participate
quizQuestions.explanation: Informed consent is an ethical requirement that participants receive sufficient information about the study to decide whether to participate voluntarily.

quizQuestions.question: Naturalistic observation requires researchers to:
options: Observe behavior without interfering in natural settings
quizQuestions.answer: Observe behavior without interfering in natural settings
quizQuestions.explanation: Naturalistic observation involves watching and recording behavior as it occurs naturally, without intervention.

quizQuestions.question: The observer effect (Hawthorne effect) refers to:
options: Behavior changing because participants know they are being observed
The difficulty of remaining objective while observing
quizQuestions.answer: Behavior changing because participants know they are being observed
quizQuestions.explanation: The Hawthorne effect occurs when awareness of being observed changes participants' behavior, threatening the naturalness of the observation.

quizQuestions.question: Inter-rater reliability in observational research refers to:
options: Whether the same observer gives consistent ratings over time
Agreement between two or more independent observers
Whether the measure captures what it intends to measure
The consistency of the observation coding scheme across studies
quizQuestions.answer: Agreement between two or more independent observers
quizQuestions.explanation: Inter-rater reliability measures the degree of agreement between independent observers using the same coding system.

quizQuestions.question: A Likert scale typically measures:
options: Degree of agreement or disagreement on a numbered scale
quizQuestions.answer: Degree of agreement or disagreement on a numbered scale
quizQuestions.explanation: Likert scales ask respondents to rate their agreement from strongly disagree to strongly agree, providing ordered response options.

quizQuestions.question: Social desirability bias in surveys refers to respondents:
options: Giving answers they believe are socially acceptable rather than truthful
quizQuestions.answer: Giving answers they believe are socially acceptable rather than truthful
quizQuestions.explanation: Social desirability bias occurs when respondents give answers that make them look good rather than answering honestly.

quizQuestions.question: A leading question in survey research is problematic because it:
options: Suggests the desired answer within the question
quizQuestions.answer: Suggests the desired answer within the question
quizQuestions.explanation: Leading questions bias responses by hinting at a desired or expected answer, compromising the validity of survey data.

quizQuestions.question: Open-ended survey questions are advantageous because they:
options: Capture a range of unexpected responses in participants' own words
quizQuestions.answer: Capture a range of unexpected responses in participants' own words
quizQuestions.explanation: Open-ended questions allow respondents to answer freely, potentially revealing unexpected themes not anticipated by the researcher.

quizQuestions.question: A correlation coefficient of +0.90 indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: A strong positive relationship
quizQuestions.explanation: Correlation coefficients close to +1.0 indicate a strong positive (direct) relationship between two variables.

quizQuestions.question: A third variable problem in correlational research means:
options: A third variable directly causes both measured variables
Three variables must always be measured simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: A third variable directly causes both measured variables
quizQuestions.explanation: The third-variable problem occurs when an unmeasured variable explains the observed correlation between two measured variables.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? A correlation coefficient of +0.90 indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: A strong positive relationship
quizQuestions.explanation: Correlation coefficients close to +1.0 indicate a strong positive (direct) relationship between two variables.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? A third variable problem in correlational research means:
options: A third variable directly causes both measured variables
Three variables must always be measured simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: A third variable directly causes both measured variables
quizQuestions.explanation: The third-variable problem occurs when an unmeasured variable explains the observed correlation between two measured variables.

quizQuestions.question: Random assignment in experiments is used to:
options: Select a representative sample from the population
Ensure all participants experience all conditions equally
Distribute individual differences evenly across experimental groups
Increase the size of the sample
quizQuestions.answer: Distribute individual differences evenly across experimental groups
quizQuestions.explanation: Random assignment distributes participant characteristics randomly across conditions, making groups equivalent before the IV is manipulated.

quizQuestions.question: In a within-subjects (repeated measures) design, each participant:
options: Is assigned to only one condition
Experiences all conditions of the experiment
Is matched with a participant in another condition
Is compared only to the control group
quizQuestions.answer: Experiences all conditions of the experiment
quizQuestions.explanation: In a within-subjects design, the same participants are tested in all conditions, reducing individual-difference confounds.

quizQuestions.question: A control group in an experiment serves as:
options: The group receiving the highest dose of the treatment
A baseline for comparison with the experimental group
The group assigned randomly to conditions
A replication of the experimental group
quizQuestions.answer: A baseline for comparison with the experimental group
quizQuestions.explanation: The control group receives no treatment (or a placebo) and provides a baseline against which to measure the effect of the independent variable.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Random assignment in experiments is used to:
options: Select a representative sample from the population
Ensure all participants experience all conditions equally
Distribute individual differences evenly across experimental groups
Increase the size of the sample
quizQuestions.answer: Distribute individual differences evenly across experimental groups
quizQuestions.explanation: Random assignment distributes participant characteristics randomly across conditions, making groups equivalent before the IV is manipulated.

quizQuestions.question: An operational definition specifies:
options: The theoretical meaning of a construct
How a variable will be measured or manipulated in a specific study
The statistical analysis to be used
The ethical justification for the study
quizQuestions.answer: How a variable will be measured or manipulated in a specific study
quizQuestions.explanation: Operational definitions translate abstract constructs into concrete, measurable procedures used in a specific study.

quizQuestions.question: Experimenter bias occurs when:
options: The researcher's expectations influence the outcome of the study
quizQuestions.answer: The researcher's expectations influence the outcome of the study
quizQuestions.explanation: Experimenter bias is the unintentional influence of a researcher's expectations on how they collect, interpret, or report data.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? An operational definition specifies:
options: The theoretical meaning of a construct
How a variable will be measured or manipulated in a specific study
The statistical analysis to be used
The ethical justification for the study
quizQuestions.answer: How a variable will be measured or manipulated in a specific study
quizQuestions.explanation: Operational definitions translate abstract constructs into concrete, measurable procedures used in a specific study.

quizQuestions.question: A simple random sample is one in which:
options: Every member of the population has an equal chance of selection
The sample is stratified by demographic groups
Participants are selected based on convenience
The researcher selects 'typical' members of the population
quizQuestions.answer: Every member of the population has an equal chance of selection
quizQuestions.explanation: In simple random sampling, all members of the population have an equal and independent probability of being selected.

quizQuestions.question: Stratified random sampling divides the population into subgroups and then:
options: Selects all members of one subgroup
Randomly samples from each subgroup in proportion to its size
quizQuestions.answer: Randomly samples from each subgroup in proportion to its size
quizQuestions.explanation: Stratified sampling ensures proportional representation of key subgroups by randomly sampling within each stratum.

quizQuestions.question: A convenience sample is a limitation because it:
options: Is too large for statistical analysis
Is unlikely to be representative of the population
Cannot be used in correlational research
quizQuestions.answer: Is unlikely to be representative of the population
quizQuestions.explanation: Convenience samples consist of participants who are easily available, which can introduce systematic bias and limit generalizability.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? A simple random sample is one in which:
options: Every member of the population has an equal chance of selection
The sample is stratified by demographic groups
Participants are selected based on convenience
The researcher selects 'typical' members of the population
quizQuestions.answer: Every member of the population has an equal chance of selection
quizQuestions.explanation: In simple random sampling, all members of the population have an equal and independent probability of being selected.

questions.question: Falsifiability means a hypothesis:
options: Can potentially be shown to be false by evidence
questions.answer: Can potentially be shown to be false by evidence
questions.explanation: Popper's criterion of falsifiability holds that scientific claims must be capable of being contradicted by observable evidence.

questions.question: Debriefing is ethically required after research involving:
questions.answer: Deception or potentially distressing procedures
questions.explanation: Debriefing is especially critical when deception or distress was involved, ensuring participants fully understand what occurred and why.

questions.question: The Hawthorne effect describes how participants:
options: Change behavior when they know they are being observed
questions.answer: Change behavior when they know they are being observed
questions.explanation: The Hawthorne effect is the alteration of behavior that occurs because individuals know they are being studied.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Social desirability bias in surveys occurs when respondents:
options: Answer in ways they believe are socially acceptable rather than fully honest
questions.answer: Answer in ways they believe are socially acceptable rather than fully honest
questions.explanation: Social desirability bias occurs when people shape answers to look acceptable or favorable.

questions.question: A scatter plot shows a tight cluster of points on a line sloping upward — this indicates:
questions.answer: A strong positive correlation
questions.explanation: A tight upward-sloping scatter indicates a strong positive relationship between the two variables.

questions.question: Random assignment ensures that:
options: The sample is representative of the population
Individual differences are evenly distributed across conditions
All participants receive the same treatment
Results will replicate in future studies
questions.answer: Individual differences are evenly distributed across conditions
questions.explanation: Random assignment makes experimental groups equivalent at the start, allowing the IV to be the explanation for any outcome differences.

questions.question: An operational definition translates an abstract variable into:
questions.answer: A specific, measurable procedure
questions.explanation: Operational definitions specify exactly how a variable will be measured or manipulated in a particular study.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Stratified random sampling is used when researchers want to:
options: Ensure important subgroups are represented in the sample
questions.answer: Ensure important subgroups are represented in the sample
questions.explanation: Stratified random sampling samples within defined subgroups so the sample reflects important population categories.

questions.question: Construct validity refers to whether a test:
options: Truly measures the psychological construct it intends to measure
questions.answer: Truly measures the psychological construct it intends to measure
questions.explanation: Construct validity evaluates whether the measurement instrument accurately taps the theoretical construct it is designed to assess.

questions.question: When data are strongly skewed, the best measure of central tendency is:
questions.answer: Median
questions.explanation: The median is resistant to outliers and is more appropriate than the mean for skewed distributions.

questions.question: In a double-blind experiment, who does NOT know which participants received the treatment?
questions.answer: Both participants and the experimenter
questions.explanation: In a double-blind design, both participants and the people administering the study are unaware of condition assignment, controlling for multiple sources of bias.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Inter-rater reliability is highest when two observers:
options: Agree closely on their independent ratings of the same event
Use different definitions for each behavior
Change the rating scale after observing
Rate different events at different times
questions.answer: Agree closely on their independent ratings of the same event
questions.explanation: High inter-rater reliability means independent observers make similar ratings using the same criteria.

questions.question: The directionality problem in correlational research means:
options: We cannot determine which variable caused the other
Three variables must always be measured
questions.answer: We cannot determine which variable caused the other
questions.explanation: Correlational data show that two variables are related but cannot establish which one is the cause and which is the effect.

questions.question: Counterbalancing in a within-subjects design controls for:
options: Order effects such as practice and fatigue
questions.answer: Order effects such as practice and fatigue
questions.explanation: Counterbalancing systematically varies the order of conditions across participants so that order effects are distributed evenly.

questions.question: The null hypothesis assumes:
options: A meaningful effect exists in the population
Any observed difference is due to chance
The study's hypothesis will be supported
questions.answer: Any observed difference is due to chance
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis is the default assumption of no effect — differences observed are attributed to random variation.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Effect size (Cohen's d) tells researchers:
options: The size or magnitude of a difference in standardized units
Whether a result is statistically significant
The number of participants in the population
questions.answer: The size or magnitude of a difference in standardized units
questions.explanation: Cohen's d describes the magnitude of a difference, which is separate from statistical significance.

questions.question: External validity is best improved by:
options: Increasing sample size of the original study
Using diverse samples and varied settings across studies
questions.answer: Using diverse samples and varied settings across studies
questions.explanation: External validity increases when findings hold across different participants, settings, and time periods.

questions.question: The APA Methods section is written with enough detail to:
options: Explain why the study is important
Allow exact replication of the study
questions.answer: Allow exact replication of the study
questions.explanation: The Methods section must provide sufficient procedural detail for independent researchers to replicate the study exactly.

questions.question: The replication crisis refers to:
options: Studies being too expensive to repeat
Many classic psychology findings failing to replicate
Statistical methods being inconsistent across labs
questions.answer: Many classic psychology findings failing to replicate
questions.explanation: Large replication projects found that a substantial proportion of published psychology findings could not be reproduced.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? A Type II error (false negative) means:
options: Failing to detect a real effect that exists
Finding an effect when none exists
questions.answer: Failing to detect a real effect that exists
questions.explanation: A Type II error occurs when a study misses a true effect. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Experimental Psychology final concepts.

questions.question: Publication bias leads to a scientific literature that:
options: Is perfectly representative of all research conducted
Favors qualitative studies over quantitative ones
questions.answer: Overrepresents statistically significant positive results
questions.explanation: Because positive findings are more likely to be published, the published literature overestimates effect sizes and the reliability of findings.

questions.question: Pre-registration of a study requires researchers to:
options: Specify hypotheses and analysis plans before data collection
Obtain IRB approval after data collection
questions.answer: Specify hypotheses and analysis plans before data collection
questions.explanation: Pre-registration transparently commits researchers to their hypotheses and analytic approaches before seeing the data.

questions.question: A 95% confidence interval that does NOT include zero suggests:
options: The result is not statistically significant
The null hypothesis cannot be evaluated
The effect is likely statistically significant
questions.answer: The effect is likely statistically significant
questions.explanation: If a 95% CI for a difference or regression coefficient excludes zero, the effect is statistically significant at α = 0.05.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? HARKing undermines scientific integrity because it:
options: Presents hypotheses created after results as if they were predicted in advance
Requires public methods before data collection
Improves transparency by separating prediction from exploration
questions.answer: Presents hypotheses created after results as if they were predicted in advance
questions.explanation: HARKing means hypothesizing after the results are known, which blurs prediction and post-hoc explanation.

questions.question: A conceptual replication tests whether:
options: The original study's procedure can be run exactly as before
The underlying theoretical claim holds using different methods or samples
Statistical analyses from the original study are correct
The original researchers' conclusions were accurate
questions.answer: The underlying theoretical claim holds using different methods or samples
questions.explanation: Conceptual replications assess the generalizability of a theoretical finding using different operationalizations or populations.

questions.question: Which sampling method is most likely to produce a representative sample?
questions.answer: Simple random sampling
questions.explanation: Simple random sampling gives every population member an equal chance of selection, minimizing systematic bias.

questions.question: The WEIRD problem highlights that most psychology research over-represents:
options: Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic samples
questions.answer: Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic samples
questions.explanation: The WEIRD critique argues that generalizing from such samples to all of humanity is problematic given their unrepresentativeness.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Internal validity is best ensured by:
options: Random assignment and experimental control of confounds
A large number of survey questions only
questions.answer: Random assignment and experimental control of confounds
questions.explanation: Internal validity is strengthened when alternative explanations are controlled, often through random assignment and careful design.

questions.question: Content validity ensures that a test:
options: Adequately samples all aspects of the construct being measured
Correlates with other accepted tests of the same construct
questions.answer: Adequately samples all aspects of the construct being measured
questions.explanation: Content validity requires that test items representatively cover the full range of the construct being assessed.

questions.question: Statistical power can be increased by:
options: Using a two-tailed test instead of one-tailed
questions.answer: Increasing sample size
questions.explanation: Larger samples reduce standard error and increase the likelihood of detecting a true effect (increasing power).

questions.question: The Discussion section of an APA report does NOT typically include:
questions.answer: Raw data tables
questions.explanation: Raw data are presented in the Results section; the Discussion interprets findings, notes limitations, and proposes future directions.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? The goal of peer review is to:
options: Evaluate a study's quality, logic, and evidence before publication
Guarantee that every conclusion is correct
questions.answer: Evaluate a study's quality, logic, and evidence before publication
questions.explanation: Peer review is a quality-control process, though it does not guarantee that a study is flawless.

questions.question: A factorial ANOVA is used when a study has:
options: Two or more IVs and one DV
Repeated measurements on a single variable
questions.answer: Two or more IVs and one DV
questions.explanation: Factorial ANOVA examines the main effects of two or more independent variables and their interactions on a single dependent variable.

questions.question: Which is the most appropriate design to study changes in reading ability from ages 6 to 16?
questions.answer: Longitudinal study
questions.explanation: A longitudinal study follows the same individuals over time, directly tracking developmental changes in reading ability.

questions.question: In Experimental Psychology final concepts, A p-value represents:
options: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability of observing results at least as extreme as found, assuming the null is true
The effect size of the study
The probability that the alternative hypothesis is true
questions.answer: The probability of observing results at least as extreme as found, assuming the null is true
questions.explanation: The p-value is the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as those observed, calculated under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true.
